title: Inherent, Processing, and Service Discontinuities
narrationScript: In the last lesson we built the ladder from indication to discontinuity to defect. This lesson is about the middle rung. Once you decide an indication is relevant, you have to name the discontinuity behind it, and the most powerful tool for naming it is knowing where discontinuities come from. Every one of them was created at one of three stages in the metal's life, and the stage predicts the shape, the location, and the orientation of what you are looking at. The three stages give us the three classes. Inherent discontinuities form while the metal is liquid or freezing, in the ingot or in the casting mold. Processing discontinuities form while solid metal is being shaped, joined, and finished, in the mill and in the shop. Service discontinuities form after the part goes to work, under load, heat, corrosion, and flow. Start at the beginning, with the melt. When an ingot freezes, the metal shrinks, and the shrinkage collects as a cavity in the top center called pipe. Dissolved gases come out of solution and leave porosity. Oxides and bits of refractory are trapped as nonmetallic inclusions, and alloying elements can freeze unevenly, which we call segregation. The mill crops away the worst of it, but whatever survives rides along into every product rolled or forged from that ingot. Castings are the other home of inherent discontinuities, and they keep them for life, which is why radiography is the classic way to examine a casting. Learn the images. Gas porosity is round, smooth, and dark. Shrinkage is dark but feathery, branching, sponge like, sitting in the heavy sections that froze last. A cold shut is a smooth dark line with rounded ends, where two streams of metal met too cold to fuse together. A hot tear is a ragged, branching dark line at a change of section, torn open while the casting was hot and weak and the mold would not let it shrink. Telling round porosity from feathery shrinkage matters, because most casting criteria grade them differently, and shrinkage points to a feeding problem back at the foundry. Now the solid metal goes to the mill, and processing begins. Rolling and forging flatten and stretch whatever the ingot left behind. Pipe and inclusions roll out into laminations, flat planar separations lying parallel to the surface of the plate. Inclusions stretch into stringers. Surface defects draw out into long tight seams. Metal folded over and pressed into a surface without fusing makes a lap, and working the metal too hard or at the wrong temperature can rupture the interior as a forging burst. Then comes welding, which is the discontinuity factory a radiographer deals with most. Porosity, slag lines along the bevels, bright tungsten specks, incomplete penetration at the root, lack of fusion along a sidewall, undercut at the toe, burn through, and cracks of every habit. After welding, the finishing operations get their chance. Quench cracks from heat treating, networks of shallow grinding cracks, machining tears, and hydrogen cracking from plating when the bake was skipped. Here is the part many new Level Twos miss. Radiography sees a discontinuity only when it changes the thickness of metal along the beam. Volumetric discontinuities, porosity, slag, shrinkage, change that thickness from any direction, so they always print. Planar ones, cracks, lack of fusion, laminations, only print when the beam runs nearly along their plane. A lamination lies flat in the plate, your beam goes straight through the thickness, the two cross at ninety degrees, and the film sees almost nothing. Laminations belong to ultrasonics. Tight cracks may need the beam lined up within a few degrees of their plane. Choosing the right method is part of classifying the discontinuity. Finally, service. The part ships clean and the world goes to work on it. Fatigue cracks start at stress raisers, thread roots, weld toes, sharp fillets, tool marks, and grow a little with every load cycle. Corrosion thins walls, digs pits, and hides under insulation. Erosion scours elbows and everything downstream of a control valve. Long years at high temperature breed creep voids. Hydrogen blisters steel, and stress corrosion cracking shows up wherever tensile stress, a susceptible alloy, and its particular corrodent meet, like chlorides on austenitic stainless steel. Radiography answers with profile shots through the insulation to measure remaining wall, and with density comparisons to gauge pit depth. But the tightest service cracks often want ultrasonics or a surface method instead. Knowing which class you are hunting, and which tool hunts it best, is exactly what makes you a Level Two.
imagePrompt: A radiographer clamps a film cassette to the far side of an insulated refinery pipe elbow while a colleague aligns a gamma projector guide tube and collimator against the weld, scaffolding and steam lines in the background.

sections.heading: Origin Tells You What to Expect
sections.body:
Every discontinuity was created at some stage of the metal's life, and that stage is the most useful single fact you can know about it. The classification is simple: **inherent** discontinuities form while the metal is being melted and solidified - in the ingot or the casting mold. **Processing** discontinuities form while solid metal is being shaped, joined, and finished - rolling, forging, extruding, welding, machining, heat treating, plating. **Service** discontinuities form after the part goes to work - under cyclic load, corrosion, temperature, and flow.

Why a Level II cares: origin predicts **shape, location, and orientation**, and those three things are what a radiographic image actually shows. If the part is a casting, expect shrinkage in the heavy sections and hot tears at the section changes. If it is rolled plate, expect any lamination to lie flat, parallel to the surface - and know that a through-thickness beam is nearly blind to it. Read the traveler before you read the film: knowing how the part was made narrows a smudge of density into a short list of suspects.

sections.caption: The three origin classes mapped to the metal's life cycle - melt and solidification, forming and joining, and operation - with the characteristic discontinuities each stage creates.

sections.heading: Inherent: Born in the Melt
sections.body:
Inherent discontinuities date from original melting and solidification. In the **ingot**, shrinkage during freezing leaves a cavity called **pipe** at the top center; dissolved gases come out of solution as **porosity** and blowholes; oxides and refractory fragments are trapped as **nonmetallic inclusions**; and alloying elements can distribute unevenly as **segregation**. Mills crop the worst of the pipe, but what survives travels into every wrought product made from that ingot.

**Castings** keep their inherent discontinuities for life, which is why radiography is the classic casting examination. Know the images:

- **Gas porosity** - smooth, round dark spots, scattered or clustered.
- **Shrinkage** - dark, feathery, branching or sponge-like regions where the last metal to freeze ran out of feed, concentrated at heavy sections and under risers.
- **Cold shut** - a smooth dark line with rounded ends where two streams of metal met too cold to fuse.
- **Hot tear** - a ragged, branching dark line at a section change, torn while the metal was hot and weak and the mold restrained its contraction.
- **Sand and slag inclusions** - irregular dark patches of mold material or dross; **misruns** and unfused **chaplets** print as gross geometry and ring-like images.

Distinguishing round gas porosity from feathery shrinkage matters: casting criteria typically grade them differently, and shrinkage signals a feeding problem that porosity does not.

sections.heading: Processing: Made in the Mill and the Shop
sections.body:
**Primary processing** - rolling, forging, drawing, extrusion - works inherent discontinuities into new shapes and adds its own. Ingot pipe and inclusions roll out flat into **laminations**, planar separations parallel to the plate surface; inclusions stretch into **stringers**; surface defects elongate into tight longitudinal **seams**; metal folded over and forced into the surface without fusing leaves a forging **lap**; and excessive reduction or working at the wrong temperature ruptures the interior as a forging **burst**.

**Welding** contributes the discontinuity family a radiographer reads most: rounded and piping **porosity**; **slag inclusions** along the bevels between passes; bright **tungsten inclusions** from GTAW; **incomplete penetration** at the root; **lack of fusion** along a sidewall; **undercut** at the toe; **burn-through**; and hot, cold, and crater **cracks** - longitudinal, transverse, or star-shaped. Their film signatures are summarized in the labeled strip below and should become reflex.

**Finishing operations** add the last group: **quench cracks** from heat treatment, deep and biased to section changes; shallow networks of **grinding cracks** from localized overheating; **machining tears** in ductile material; and **hydrogen embrittlement cracking** after plating or pickling when the bake-out was skipped. A part is not safe from new discontinuities until it ships - and, as the next section shows, not even then.

sections.caption: Simulated weld radiograph with the classic film signatures: round dark porosity, irregular slag along the bevel, a dead-straight incomplete-penetration line, a fine wandering crack, bright tungsten spots, and dark undercut at the toe.

sections.title: Orientation decides what RT can see
sections.body: Radiography excels at **volumetric** discontinuities - porosity, slag, shrinkage - because they change the metal thickness along the beam from any angle. **Planar** discontinuities - cracks, lack of fusion, laminations - print only when the beam runs roughly along their plane. A lamination lies flat in the plate, so a beam shot through the thickness crosses it at ninety degrees and records almost no density change: laminations are a job for ultrasonics. Tight service cracks may likewise demand beam alignment within a few degrees - or a surface method to back RT up.

sections.heading: Service: Earned in Operation
sections.body:
Service discontinuities initiate and grow while the part works. **Fatigue cracks** start at stress concentrations - thread roots, sharp fillets, weld toes, tool marks, even arc strikes - and advance with every load cycle until the remaining section fails fast; on film they are tight, fine, and easily missed without favorable beam alignment. **Corrosion** appears as general wall thinning, isolated **pitting**, crevice attack at joints, and the notorious hidden attack under insulation. **Erosion** and flow-accelerated thinning polish away elbows, tees, and anything downstream of an orifice or control valve. Long exposure to high temperature produces **creep** damage - grain-boundary voids that link into cracks late in life. **Hydrogen damage** can blister steel or attack it at temperature, and **stress-corrosion cracking** - the combination of tensile stress, a susceptible alloy, and its specific corrodent, such as chlorides on austenitic stainless steel - produces fine, branched cracking.

In-service radiography is built around these mechanisms: **profile (tangential) shots** image the pipe wall edge-on through insulation on an operating line to measure remaining thickness, and pit depth is estimated by comparing the density through the pit with the adjacent full wall. The tightest service cracks, though, are frequently better served by UT, MT, or PT - knowing when RT is the wrong tool is Level II knowledge too.

sections.caption: The three origin classes at a glance.
rows: Melting and solidification - ingot or casting mold
Ingot pipe, gas porosity, nonmetallic inclusions, segregation, shrinkage, cold shuts, hot tears
Round dark spots; feathery branching shrinkage; smooth dark cold-shut lines; ragged hot tears at section changes
Forming, joining, and finishing - mill and shop
Laminations, stringers, seams, laps, bursts; weld porosity, slag, tungsten, IP, lack of fusion, cracks; quench and grinding cracks
Weld signatures per the labeled strip; laminations nearly invisible to a through-thickness beam
In operation - load, environment, temperature, flow
Fatigue cracks, corrosion and pitting, erosion, creep, hydrogen damage, stress-corrosion cracking
Tight fine cracks needing beam alignment; pits as sharp-edged dark spots; wall loss read by profile shots and density comparison

figures.title: Where discontinuities are born: the metal's life cycle
How common weld discontinuities print on film

interactive.title: Classify-the-Discontinuity Drill
interactive.intro: Drop each discontinuity into the life-cycle stage that created it. Think about where the metal was when the damage happened - still freezing, being shaped and joined, or already working for a living.

items.label: Cold shut where two streams of casting metal met without fusing
items.why: Formed during pouring and solidification - the streams were too cold to fuse. Inherent.

items.label: Feathery shrinkage in the heavy section of a casting
items.why: The last metal to freeze ran out of feed liquid - a solidification event in the mold. Inherent.

items.label: Nonmetallic inclusions strung through an ingot
items.why: Oxides and refractory trapped during the original melt - inherent, and the ancestor of stringers and laminations downstream.

items.label: Lamination lying parallel to the surface of rolled plate
items.why: Rolling flattened ingot pipe and inclusions into a planar separation - created by primary processing even though its raw material was inherent.

items.label: Forging lap folded into a flange surface
items.why: Metal folded over and pressed into the surface without fusing during forging - a primary-processing discontinuity.

items.label: Quench crack at a section change after heat treatment
items.why: Thermal stress from the quench tore the part - heat treatment is a finishing (processing) operation.

items.label: Tungsten inclusion in a GTAW root pass
items.why: Dropped into the weld by the electrode - welding is a processing operation, and tungsten prints as a bright light spot.

items.label: Fatigue crack growing from a sharp thread root
items.why: Initiated and grown by cyclic loading in operation - the defining service discontinuity.

items.label: Pitting under the insulation of an operating line
items.why: Corrosion attacking the part in service - found with profile shots and density comparison.

items.label: Branched chloride cracking in austenitic stainless steel
items.why: Stress-corrosion cracking needs tensile stress, a susceptible alloy, and its corrodent - a combination that occurs in service.

checkQuestions.question: A smooth dark line with rounded ends appears in a casting radiograph where two streams of metal met during pouring. This is:
options: A hot tear - a processing discontinuity
A cold shut - an inherent discontinuity
Lack of fusion - a processing discontinuity
A fatigue crack - a service discontinuity
checkQuestions.explanation: A cold shut forms when two melt streams meet too cold to fuse - an inherent solidification discontinuity. Its smooth line and rounded ends distinguish it from the ragged, branching hot tear.

checkQuestions.question: Why does radiography perform poorly on laminations in rolled plate?
options: Laminations are always too small to resolve on film
They lie parallel to the plate surface, so a through-thickness beam crosses them at ninety degrees and sees almost no thickness change
Lead screens filter out the radiation that would image them
Laminations occur only at plate edges outside the film coverage
checkQuestions.explanation: A lamination is planar and parallel to the surface; the beam crosses its plane at right angles, so it presents essentially no change in transmitted thickness. Ultrasonics, with its beam normal to the lamination, finds them easily.

checkQuestions.question: In a casting radiograph, smooth round scattered dark spots versus dark feathery branching regions distinguish:
options: Gas porosity from shrinkage
Sand inclusions from segregation
A cold shut from a misrun
A hot tear from a forging burst
checkQuestions.explanation: Round smooth spots are gas porosity; feathery, branching, sponge-like dark regions are shrinkage. Criteria typically grade them differently, and shrinkage points to a feeding problem in the foundry.

checkQuestions.question: A deep crack found at a sharp section change after heat treatment of a machined part is classified as:
options: Inherent - it traces back to the ingot
Service-induced - cracks only grow in operation
Processing - a quench crack from a finishing operation
Nonrelevant - heat-treat marks are surface conditions
checkQuestions.explanation: Quench cracks form when thermal stresses from rapid cooling tear the part, typically at section changes - heat treatment is a finishing step, so the discontinuity is a processing discontinuity.

checkQuestions.question: Stress-corrosion cracking requires which combination of conditions?
options: Cyclic loading, high temperature, and long time at stress
Tensile stress, a susceptible alloy, and its specific corrosive environment
Hydrogen charging, low temperature, and high pressure
Compressive stress and any corrodent
checkQuestions.explanation: SCC is the three-legged stool of tensile stress, susceptible material, and the matching corrodent - chlorides on austenitic stainless steel is the classic pairing - and it produces fine, branched cracking.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Film Quality and Manufacturing Processes Course 3.2.1-3.2.3
Standard NDT terminology for discontinuity classes (ASTM E1316 style, general reference)
Reference radiograph collections for castings (ASTM E446-family, general reference)
Weld discontinuity acceptance concepts in fabrication codes (AWS D1.1 / ASME B31 style, general reference)
Fitness-for-service concepts for service damage mechanisms (API 579 / ASME FFS style, general reference)
